stdout:
OBSERVATIONS:
- Image 1 (texture/color depth): the lime-green body color is essentially flat across the whole frame — no visible striation bands or brightness gradient tied to the rolling texture. Real hand-rolled sheet glass backlit like this typically shows streaky light/dark banding from thickness variation; here only the bubbles break up an otherwise uniform field.
- Image 1 (noise/optics): the bubble edges are high-contrast dark rings on a saturated bright field, a classic spot for chromatic aberration in real macro photography, but every bubble edge is clean neutral-gray/black with no purple/green fringing.
- Image 1 (lighting): illumination is perfectly even edge-to-edge with no falloff or hot spot, consistent with either a very well-diffused light table or a synthetic uniform environment — no distinguishing gradient either way.
- Image 2 (front-surface reflection/texture): the blue sheet carries a clear reflected/refracted image of the house that is visibly warped into wavy bands (window frames bend and repeat), which is the signature optical fingerprint of real rolled/rippled glass distorting a reflection.
- Image 2 (color depth/lighting): the red sheet has a bright diffuse highlight streak near the top-left that fades unevenly downward, with local darkening mottling tied to the visible surface waviness — brightness is coupled to texture rather than flat.
- Image 2 (edge effect): the top physical edge of both sheets shows an irregular, slightly darker rim following the uneven hand-cut boundary, plausible for real glass.
- Image 2 (optics): the reflected house detail is slightly softer/lower-contrast than the glass sheet's own surface plane, consistent with a real secondary focal/reflection plane rather than a single flat render pass.

VERDICT: Image 1 synthetic -- its color/brightness stays flat and texture-decoupled with no chromatic aberration on hard bubble edges, unlike Image 2 which shows texture-coupled luminance, wavy reflection distortion, and edge/optics behavior typical of real glass.
